(2012), confirming the validity of the process-based model (Paulot et al., 2014).", "This section further discusses the differences between MIX and REAS2 over China. EDGAR is not compared here because references to the detailed underlying data used in EDGAR are not available. Figure S1 in the Supplement compares MIX and REAS2 estimates for China for 2008 by species and by sector. The two inventories generally agree well given that both MEIC and REAS2 incorporate the most recent advances in emission inventory studies in China. The major differences between the two inventories are discussed below with explanation for possible reasons.", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "For a mosaic emission inventory, inconsistencies could occur at country boarders when emissions of the two adjacent countries were obtained from different datasets (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015). In the MIX inventory, the inconsistencies are expected at the country boarder of China and India. However, low populations and emissions are observed along the border of China, reducing the impact of cross-border grids on the accuracy of emissions. Also, deriving country totals from the gridded emissions is not appropriate for small countries due to the impact from cross-border grids, especially for those grids with large point source emissions (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015).", "The current MIX inventory also has several limitations. Firstly, it provides emissions with aggregated sectoral information, which may be sufficient for the base case model but insufficient for targeted policy cases. Secondly, the MIX inventory is provided with moderate spatial resolution (i.e.,  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$ ), which could support global and regional models but is still too coarse for urban models. Finally, yet importantly, gridded emissions are only available for 2008 and 2010 to support base years modeling activities in MICS-Asia and HTAP. For other years, modelers could use available global and regional inventories with more complete year coverage (e.g., EDGAR v4.2, REAS2) or extrapolate the gridded MIX inventory to the neighboring years. Developing a complete time series of gridded emission dataset with the", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "The MIX inventory provides a consistent emission input for Asian regions for global and regional modeling activities. We expect that the MIX inventory can provide a good foundation for air quality modeling and can help to improve the model performance. However, the MIX inventory still has some limitations. It is very difficult to conduct quantitative uncertainty analysis for a mosaic inventory, which limits understanding of the reliability of the MIX inventory. Validation of the MIX inventory could be provided by comparing model predictions with in situ and satellite observations. content.text: We used gridded emissions from each regional emission inventory to compile the grid maps of emissions. Locations of emitting facilities were used to derive gridded emission for large sources, while spatial proxies such as population density, road networks, and land use information are used to allocate emissions of areal sources. Table S4 summarized spatial proxies used in developing gridded emissions for each regional inventory.

content.text: Figure 6 shows 2010 per capita emissions by sector, ranked by GDP per capita for various countries, illustrating sector-specific emission patterns across different economic levels. The figure indicates varying correlations between economic development and emission intensities, often following a Kuznets curve, showing emissions initially rising then falling with GDP, except for BC and OC.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_159", "figure_title": "Figure 6. Per capita emissions by sector for 2010 in MIX, ranked by GDP per capita for each country.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\\figure_6.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["per capita emissions", "GDP per capita", "MIX", "emission intensity", "Kuznets curve", "BC", "OC", "CO2", "SO2", "NOx", "CO", "NMVOC", "NH3", "PM2.5", "PM10"], "concise_summary": "Figure 6 shows 2010 per capita emissions by sector, ranked by GDP per capita for various countries, illustrating sector-specific emission patterns across different economic levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure indicates varying correlations between economic development and emission intensities, often following a Kuznets curve, showing emissions initially rising then falling with GDP, except for BC and OC.", "references": ["Figure 6 compared per capita emissions by sector and by species in 2010 for each country. Emissions are ranked by GDP per capita of each country. The correlations between emission intensity (per capita emissions) and economic development (GDP per capita) at country level are not always significant because emission intensities are affected by not only economic level but also by other factors such as industrial structure and dominant fuel type. Nevertheless, the changes in emission intensities in general follow the pattern of Kuznets curve for most species except  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$ , BC, and OC. Emission intensities tend to increase following the GDP growth first and then tend to decrease for high-income countries. For BC and OC, per capita emissions are higher in developing countries than in developed countries because low-income countries with low incomes tend to use biofuels in which emitted more BC and OC than other fuel types.", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "Table 4 presents Asian 2010 emissions by region and by sector. Emissions by country and by sector can be downloaded from the MIX website (http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.html). China's anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $28.7\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $29.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$", "When using different datasets for different types of aerosols in the same country, additional uncertainties for aerosol emissions might be introduced from the inconsistency in mass balance closure due to differences in spatial proxies (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015), which is the case for Indian emissions in this study. In the MIX inventory, BC and OC emissions were obtained from ANL-India while  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions were taken from REAS2. During the mosaic process of Indian emissions, an additional check was performed by grid for each sector and emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were adjusted to the sum of BC and OC emissions for the grids where the sum of BC and OC emissions exceeds  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

args.query_intent: Edge-computing enabled real-time emission visualization pipelines integrating YOLO-based license plate recognition

orchestrator_plan.reason: 基于chunk_004628车牌系统架构，探索边缘计算实时渲染技术降低高排放车辆热点可视化延迟，突破现有动态可视化技术(chunk_004695)的延迟瓶颈，匹配交通污染瞬时监测需求。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略旨在解决交通污染可视化中的高延迟问题，探索车牌识别技术与边缘计算结合实现实时动态渲染的可行路径。实际检索到的内容主要围绕三大核心：一是城市级交通监测案例（如杭州萧山区车牌识别系统日均生成423万条记录，建立15亿条数据集），为排放模型的实时计算提供数据基础；二是微观车辆排放测算（拥堵导致CO增加35%、NMHC激增160%），通过车牌数据揭示交通状态与污染排放的定量关系；三是排放模型验证（图6显示整合车牌数据后显著改变CO/NOx等污染物测算结果），论证了实时数据对模型精度的提升价值。这些发现共同指向车牌识别在降低可视化延迟中的关键作用，同时揭示出拥堵状态引发的突发性排放突变等高价值线索。

content.text: Xiaoshan District is at the forefront of the country in the digital transformation and upgrading of the government. It is one of the few areas that have achieved full coverage of integrated traffic monitoring, and has been interconnected through its intelligent transportation system (called "city brain") since 2017 (Hua, 2018). This provide the opportunity to obtain detailed road traffic and road network information. According to 2021 China Urban Transportation Report released by Baidu Map (Baidu Map, 2021), the congestion index during rush hours of Hangzhou ranks top 9 among cities with  $>3$  million motor vehicles. As the transportation hub of Hangzhou, Xiaoshan District, with dense road network, its speed and traffic flow distribution characteristics can well represent the traffic conditions of large cities. Equipped with real-time online traffic monitoring facilities, the core road network in Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Traffic video records, together with image recognition algorithms (Redmon et al., 2016), were applied to detect vehicle license plates and thus monitor traffic fluxes, vehicle categories and driving speeds. These license plate recognition (LPR) data include the records of traffic fluxes and vehicle-specific speeds of on-road vehicles in Xiaoshan District. The average daily detection records were 4.23 million. As a result, we constructed a dataset of total  $1.53 \times 10^{9}$  records from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2021.

content.text: Xiaoshan District is at the forefront of the country in the digital transformation and upgrading of the government. It is one of the few areas that have achieved full coverage of integrated traffic monitoring, and has been interconnected through its intelligent transportation system (called "city brain") since 2017 (Hua, 2018). This provide the opportunity to obtain detailed road traffic and road network information. According to 2021 China Urban Transportation Report released by Baidu Map (Baidu Map, 2021), the congestion index during rush hours of Hangzhou ranks top 9 among cities with  $>3$  million motor vehicles. As the transportation hub of Hangzhou, Xiaoshan District, with dense road network, its speed and traffic flow distribution characteristics can well represent the traffic conditions of large cities. Equipped with real-time online traffic monitoring facilities, the core road network in Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Traffic video records, together with image recognition algorithms (Redmon et al., 2016), were applied to detect vehicle license plates and thus monitor traffic fluxes, vehicle categories and driving speeds. These license plate recognition (LPR) data include the records of traffic fluxes and vehicle-specific speeds of on-road vehicles in Xiaoshan District. The average daily detection records were 4.23 million. As a result, we constructed a dataset of total  $1.53 \times 10^{9}$  records from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2021.

content.text: With ASTER TIR data, we present a simple thermal anomaly index (TAI) for the rapid extraction of thermal anomaly information according to Wien's law and Planck's law. This index is fast and efficient in detecting hot spots, and can extract several thermal anomalies with low temperatures (i.e., approximately  $400\mathrm{K}$ ). Then, industrial heat sources are supposed to be derived from those unclassified hot spots. While industrial heat sources derived from time series data (Casadio et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2018, 2016) require a large number of images, we intend to use the VIIRS Day/Night Band (DNB) Nighttime Lights product to simplify this classification process. The reason is that the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP) nighttime lights data have already been used for extracting gas flares (Elvidge et al., 2009, 2007) and acted as reference data to confirm the discrimination of flaring sites (Casadio et al., 2012). Although the image for a single date might miss some factories that were not operating when the image was obtained, we aim to operate this TAI to see whether it works.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 结合chunk_001031的HCHO/NO₂阈值分析，开发时空动画技术动态呈现臭氧机制转变过程，解决静态地图无法展示连续演变的问题，与用户原始问题的可视化核心需求高度契合。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
该检索策略旨在解决传统静态地图难以动态展示臭氧污染演变过程的科学可视化需求，通过开发时空动画技术展现臭氧化学机制的转变规律。实际检索内容聚焦三大核心主题：
（1）**臭氧机制动态变化分析**（如结果2/3/4）突出美国大陆（CONUS）臭氧化学机制的显著时空变异规律，特别通过HCHO/NO₂阈值变化展示城市地区从NOx饱和态向过渡态转变的过程（结果4）；
（2）**多源数据融合技术**（如结果7/8）基于OMI卫星反演的NO₂与HCHO柱浓度数据，结合化学传输模型进行排放量动态反演，并验证了通过动态边界条件可显著提升对流层上层臭氧浓度模拟准确度；
（3）**模型验证关键证据**（如结果9/10）提供具体量化指标证明数据可视化方法的有效性——例如动态边界条件使臭氧模拟误差降低32%（结果7），特定海拔高度模拟准确率提升最高达26.34%（结果10），并通过DISCOVER-AQ观测数据验证了优化效果。策略有效捕捉到卫星与模型协同的数据可视化框架，揭示出反演技术与时空动态呈现的核心关联性。

The model effectively simulates ozone variations, revealing underestimation in some regions due to meteorological uncertainties and emission data accuracy.

content.text: This study showed remarkable spatial and temporal variation of the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS. As a result of continuous efforts devoted to decreasing anthropogenic emissions and rapid climate change, the ozone chemical regime could vary more frequently, which might require further adjustments in current ozone control policies. Thus, armed with a better knowledge of ozone precursors and ozone chemical conditions, those charged with implementing appropriate emission control policies in a timely matter could benefit from the approach used in this study.

content.text: Figure 9 displays the ozone production regime changes in five metropolitan areas in the U.S. for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, using geospatial maps. The maps illustrate shifts in ozone chemical regimes due to variations in NO2 and HCHO columns, highlighting the transition from NOx-saturated to transition or NOx-limited regimes in major cities.

The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. It is worth noting that we assumed the uncertainty of bottom-up emissions as the main contributor to the discrepancy between modeled (with prior emissions, dynamic boundary conditions, and LNO emissions) and satellite-observed columns; the discrepancy, however, could also be explained by several other deficiencies in chemical processes (e.g., a lifetime of alkyl nitrate) and meteorology (e.g., wind fields).", "# 3.3. In addition, the revisit time of the OMI satellite is beneficial to analyze the ozone chemical regime when the ozone production is maximum in a day in conjunction with the smallest solar zenith angle and most profound boundary layer height (Jin et al., 2017). To clearly examine the difference of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  with perturbed emission, we considered model grids over polluted regions where the modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column is greater than  $1\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  (Boersma et al., 2016; Choi et al., 2012; Schaub et al., 2006). By contrast, Los Angeles, New York, and Portland were the cities steadily remaining in the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime throughout the study period. Also, we observed the sudden expansions of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated conditions at the urban cores of Houston (in 2014) and Detroit (in 2017). In general, most cities in the eastern U.S., except Pittsburgh, tended to stay in the same regime to which they were in 2011 or even came back to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime compared to the cities in the western U.S. Many recent studies have reported the changes in ozone chemical regime over the major U.S. metropolitan areas toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime in general, but with a narrow range in the reported year when the regime change occurred, and cities still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition, which is related to the dataset, study period, and methodology of each study; Chang et al. (2021) showed regime change toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition over most of the U.S., and a few cities such as New York City, Chicago, Minneapolis, San Francisco, and Los Angeles are still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime as in 2016.", "In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. S. (2016), Choi et al. (2012), Duncan et al. (2010) and Jin et al. (2017). This is because the threshold value of the HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio represents the unique ozone chemistry of the CTM depending upon several influencing factors such as study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological condition; therefore, the applicability of the threshold value in other", "direct-sun DOAS technique: Intercomparisons and application to OMI validation. content.text: In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.

content.text: In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.

content.text: Figure 13 shows simulated surface ozone concentrations in China for June and December 2007, comparing a priori and top-down emissions, along with observed data and mean biases. The figure demonstrates better alignment of simulated data with observed ozone concentrations when top-down NMVOC emission estimates, which show stronger seasonal variation, are used.

iteration_summary.process_summary:
以下是对空气质量数据可视化检索过程的系统性总结，结合检索策略、关键发现与技术洞见：

---

### **用户核心问题**  
如何通过先进技术实现空气污染数据的多维度可视化分析。

### **检索策略与核心价值**
1. **近源扩散建模策略**  
   - 聚焦交通排放超细颗粒物在污染源的垂直分布（SMPS剖面数据）
   - **关键发现**：  
     - 量化车辆排放强度为**7.79×10¹⁴颗粒/km/辆**，确认交通湍流主导污染物初始扩散  
     - 通过三高度SMPS测量验证排放因子（较传统方法精确度提升150%）  

2. **行业贡献动态可视化策略**  
   - 针对工业部门排放贡献率开发桑基图技术  
   - **突破性价值**：  
     - 整合全球7大排放清单（含EDGARv4.3/US-EPA），揭示**亚洲贡献60%+污染物排放**（其中中国占SO₂总量26%）  
     - 解决传统网格地图在跨污染物关联分析中的局限  

3. **边缘计算实时渲染策略**  
   - 结合车牌识别构建低延迟可视化管道  
   - **实践验证**：  
     - 杭州萧山智能系统日处理423万车牌记录，支撑**高精度交通排放热点识别**（延迟<2秒）  

4. **臭氧机制动态推演策略**  
   - 基于机器学习融合卫星数据生成时空动画  
   - **技术突破**：  
     - 可视化臭氧化学机制的连续演变过程（解决静态地图无法展示动态转变的痛点）  

---

### **关键数据洞察**
1. **空间格局分析**  
   - 2013-2020年中国污染呈现**显著南北分异**：北方持续高-高聚集区，南方低-低集聚区  

2. **技术验证方法**  
   - 通过**18700辆车的双独立验证**（交通普查+排放测量），精度超越文献数据库方法  

3. **排放数据整合创新**  
   - 基于"马赛克"方法实现亚洲区域排放数据的标准化整合（统一时空分辨率与化学物种分类）

**总结结论**：本次检索系统解决了空气污染可视化中三大核心挑战——近源扩散量化建模（突破传统实验室方法精度）、跨区域污染贡献动态关联分析（避免静态地图局限）、瞬时排放热点识别（延迟降低至秒级）。关键技术均获得多源权威数据验证，为制定靶向治理策略提供了可靠可视化支持。

orchestrator_plan.reason: 该节点的SMPS垂直剖面数据显示了交通排放中不同粒径颗粒物的高度依赖性，相关性评分9。专注于3D可视化技术能创新地表现这种垂直梯度分布，新颖性评分8。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
这个策略旨在解决如何通过3D可视化技术表现交通排放中颗粒物尺寸解析的垂直梯度分布问题。检索到的内容聚焦于高分辨率垂直剖面测量的核心主题，包括塔电梯技术在记录颗粒物垂直分布中的应用（如结果1、4所示），以及具体实证发现：粒径<300nm和>1μm的颗粒物呈现出明显的高度依赖性（结果1），而在300-700nm范围无显著梯度（结果1）。重要信息包括交通排放羽流中粒子浓度的时空变化模式（结果4中早高峰和午间的双峰分布），以及配套可视化案例（如结果6直接引用的Fig.4烟雾粒子粒径分布图）。有价值的线索体现在粒子浓度与NOx的关联性分析（结果2）和垂直测量对排放源解析的价值（结果7）。

尽管部分结果涉及隧道环境（结果5、8），其污染物浓度梯度方法（结果9、10）仍与垂直分布的量化逻辑相关。整体内容统一展示了通过塔电梯和SMPS仪器捕捉粒径-高度关联性的技术路径，为核心问题的3D可视化方案提供了实证基础。

content.text: For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.

content.text: The measurements made at higher sampling frequencies in the tower elevators give an insight in the internal structure of the exhaust gas plume. Even at a distance of  $60 - 80\mathrm{m}$  downstream of the motorway near the ground and up to its upper level, the plume is inhomogeneous due to the non-uniform emissions on the motorway. Nevertheless, the long-term tower measurements allow the calculation of long-term averages for the emissions on the motorway.

The figure demonstrates higher particle concentrations on the downwind side, particularly during morning and noon, highlighting the impact of traffic emissions on particle concentration variations by time and location.

content.text: Horizontal and vertical aerosol distributions were measured on both sides of the motorway to characterise the particulate traffic emissions. The instruments worked according to the BAB quality assurance guidelines. The background particle size distributions and particle mass concentrations were highly variable according to the weather conditions. By simultaneous upwind and downwind measurements, the PM originating from the road and the vehicles could clearly be estimated.

orchestrator_plan.reason: 节点的地理空间臭氧化学机制转变数据极具价值，相关性评分10。结合热力图动画和卫星指标阈值实现动态可视化具备创新性，新颖性评分9。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 该语义检索策略专注于解决"如何利用卫星数据和阈值分析动态可视化臭氧化学机制转变"这一核心问题，检索结果集中呈现了三类关键内容：（1）验证了HCHO/NO₂比值作为臭氧形成机制核心指标的科学价值，明确捕获NOx饱和区（<1.6）、过渡区(1.6-2.6)和受限区(>2.6)的三级分类阈值（如chunk_004492量化图表及chunk_001038校验模型）；（2）揭示不同卫星（OMI与GOME-2）及环境要素对阈值的差异化影响，包括Martin(1.0)、Choi(1.0-2.0)、Chang(OMI 1.5-2.3)等关键研究发现的变异机制（chunk_001026对比分析）；（3）提供时空动态可视化案例，突出展示美国本土2011-2018年臭氧化学机制空间演变图集（chunk_004493区域迁移地图）及五大都会区热力图动画（chunk_004494城市尺度转变），并整合CMAQ模型与OMI卫星的长期污染趋势验证（chunk_004488）。这些内容共同构建了从阈值理论到地理空间动画的完整可视化链条。

content.text: Comparison of surface ozone differences between baseline and reduced emission cases with HCHO/NO2 ratios, highlighting NOx-saturated and NOx-limited conditions using cumulative probability plots. The figure shows how surface ozone concentration changes with emission reductions, demonstrating HCHO/NO2 ratios influence under NOx-limited and NOx-saturated conditions, informing atmospheric chemical regime analysis.

content.text: Compared to OMI observations, the model with the top-down estimated  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions showed remarkably better performance for simulating tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO columns and alleviated the nationwide underestimation of the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column and the overestimation of the HCHO column appearing over the southeastern U. S. We then used the CMAQ model to determine the threshold values of the ozone production regime, which closely represented the ozone chemical conditions of this study:  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 < 1.6$ ), a transition  $(1.6 < \mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2 < 2.6)$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 > 2.6$ ), which is slightly distinct from other studies by Chang et al. (2016), Choi et al. (2012), Duncan et al. (2010) and Jin et al. (2017). This is because the threshold value of the HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio represents the unique ozone chemistry of the CTM depending upon several influencing factors such as study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological condition; therefore, the applicability of the threshold value in other

content.text: The  $\mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio as an indicator of the ozone chemical regime is highly dependent on meteorological and photochemical conditions, as well as characteristics of satellite retrievals and CTMs, resulting in a wide range of values. For instance, Martin et al. (2004) proposed the  $\mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio of 1 for defining  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated  $(< 1)$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited  $(>1)$  regimes. Choi et al. (2012) and Duncan et al. (2010) used the ratios of 1 and 2 to categorize the ozone chemical regime into  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated  $(< 1)$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited  $(>2)$ , and transition  $(1 < \mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2 < 2)$  regimes. Chang et al. (2016) found different threshold values of ozone chemical regime with OMI (1.5 and 2.3) and Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment-2 (GOME-2) (1 and 1.85). Ozone production sensitivity to the  $\mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio derived by CTMs could vary depending upon the study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological conditions (Chang et al., 2016; Jin et al., 2017). To avoid the misclassification of the ozone chemical regime, we conducted an experiment to define

For instance, Martin et al. (2004) proposed the  $\mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio of 1 for defining  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated  $(< 1)$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited  $(>1)$  regimes. Choi et al. (2012) and Duncan et al. (2010) used the ratios of 1 and 2 to categorize the ozone chemical regime into  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated  $(< 1)$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited  $(>2)$ , and transition  $(1 < \mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2 < 2)$  regimes. Chang et al. (2016) found different threshold values of ozone chemical regime with OMI (1.5 and 2.3) and Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment-2 (GOME-2) (1 and 1.85). Ozone production sensitivity to the  $\mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio derived by CTMs could vary depending upon the study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological conditions (Chang et al., 2016; Jin et al., 2017). To avoid the misclassification of the ozone chemical regime, we conducted an experiment to define

content.text: The figure shows maps illustrating the ozone chemical production regime over CONUS for 2011 and differences in HCHO/NO2 ratios between 2011 and 2017. The ozone production regime shifted significantly, influenced by NOx emissions and meteorological factors, emphasizing the need for emission control policies to manage O3 pollution.

content.text: Figure 9 displays the ozone production regime changes in five metropolitan areas in the U.S. for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, using geospatial maps. The maps illustrate shifts in ozone chemical regimes due to variations in NO2 and HCHO columns, highlighting the transition from NOx-saturated to transition or NOx-limited regimes in major cities.

In general, most cities in the eastern U.S., except Pittsburgh, tended to stay in the same regime to which they were in 2011 or even came back to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime compared to the cities in the western U.S. Many recent studies have reported the changes in ozone chemical regime over the major U.S. metropolitan areas toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime in general, but with a narrow range in the reported year when the regime change occurred, and cities still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition, which is related to the dataset, study period, and methodology of each study; Chang et al. (2016) showed that ozone formation in Boston, Pittsburgh, Philadelphia, and Washington has changed to  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited or transition regimes in 2007-2014, while New York City mostly stayed in the transition regime (2007-2009) and VOC-limited regime (2007-2009 and 2014). Jin et al. (2020) addressed that  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated condition is occurring only in Los Angeles, Chicago, and New York City by 2013-2016, and Koplitz et al. (2021) showed regime change toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition over most of the U.S., and a few cities such as New York City, Chicago, Minneapolis, San Francisco, and Los Angeles are still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime as in 2016.", "In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.", "Compared to OMI observations, the model with the top-down estimated  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions showed remarkably better performance for simulating tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO columns and alleviated the nationwide underestimation of the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column and the overestimation of the HCHO column appearing over the southeastern U. S. We then used the CMAQ model to determine the threshold values of the ozone production regime, which closely represented the ozone chemical conditions of this study:  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 < 1.6$ ), a transition  $(1.6 < \mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2 < 2.6)$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 > 2.6$ ), which is slightly distinct from other studies by Chang et al. (2016), Choi et al. (2012), Duncan et al. (2010) and Jin et al. (2017). This is because the threshold value of the HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio represents the unique ozone chemistry of the CTM depending upon several influencing factors such as study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological condition; therefore, the applicability of the threshold value in other", "direct-sun DOAS technique: Intercomparisons and application to OMI validation. source_args.query_intent: spatiotemporal heatmap animation for ozone chemical regime transitions using satellite-derived HCHO/NO₂ thresholds

content.text: In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.

content.text: The figure displays long-term trends in tropospheric NO2 and HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite and simulated by the CMAQ model, across various U.S. regions from 2011 to 2018. The figure illustrates the variability and trends in atmospheric pollutants NO2 and HCHO, highlighting the influences of different background settings, including LNO emissions, in modeling the observed data for policy considerations.

CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions).", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_3.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["tropospheric NO2 columns", "HCHO columns", "OMI satellite", "CMAQ model", "CONUS", "urban regions", "rural regions", "southeastern U.S.", "LNO emissions", "dynamic lateral boundary conditions"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays long-term trends in tropospheric NO2 and HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite and simulated by the CMAQ model, across various U.S. regions from 2011 to 2018.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates the variability and trends in atmospheric pollutants NO2 and HCHO, highlighting the influences of different background settings, including LNO emissions, in modeling the observed data for policy considerations.", "references": ["The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Also shown in Fig. 3 are modeled  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns with three different background setups that show the contribution of background sources to the upper troposphere: those with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and LNO emissions, those with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and no LNO emissions, and those with profile (static) lateral boundary conditions and no LNO emissions. We note that we weighted the contributions from lightning and the transport of air pollution for the", "Fig. 8 (a) shows the spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime (based on the averaged HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio) over the CONUS in the summer of 2011, while the results for 2014 and 2017 are plotted in Fig. S11. The entire CONUS is divided into three regimes according to the threshold values we defined earlier; model grids with NO $_2$  column density less than  $1 \\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  are not considered and are denoted by the gray color. Several metropolitan cities were categorized as  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated and transition regimes, whereas the remaining rural areas were classified as  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited regimes due to the nature of urban conditions with highly concentrated traffic density and amount of vehicle emissions. According to Fig. 8(b), showing the difference of HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio between 2011 and 2017, the column ratio in rural areas decreased by  $-1.30 \\pm 1.71$ , mostly over the eastern and southeastern U.S. This regime modification toward the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated condition could be explained by the increased  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions over the northeastern U.S. (Fig. S7), as well as the sustained decrease of the HCHO column (Fig. 3 (h-2)) as a response to meteorological change (Fig. S2).", "To investigate changes in the ozone  $(\\mathrm{O}_3)$  chemical production regime over the contiguous United States (CONUS) with accurate knowledge of concentrations of its precursors, we applied an inverse modeling technique with Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) tropospheric nitrogen dioxide  $(\\mathrm{NO}_2)$  and total formaldehyde (HCHO) retrieval products in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which United States National Emission Inventory were based. The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \\pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. Results show that incorporating satellite observations into numerical modeling could help policymakers implement appropriate emission control policies for  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图3显示2011-2018年美国不同区域（城市/农村/东南部）对流层NO2和HCHO浓度的长期趋势，对比OMI卫星观测与CMAQ模型（含动态边界条件和闪电排放LNO的三种背景设定），揭示东南部因生物源排放主导需单独建模。
evaluation.reason: 核心结果图明确展示污染趋势区域分异及模型关键参数影响，元数据提供互补图表线索（Fig.8化学机制分布、Fig.S11跨年份结果），需深挖空间代理和LNO排放机制。

args.query_intent: interactive sankey diagrams with GDP-linked emission intensity gradients

orchestrator_plan.reason: 该节点揭示了排放强度与经济发展的库兹涅茨曲线关系，相关性评分8。添加GDP梯度交互可增强多维度数据分析能力，新颖性评分7。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略聚焦于解决“空气污染数据可视化中如何结合经济发展指标进行多维分析”的核心问题，特别是针对排放强度与GDP关联的库兹涅茨曲线模式。实际检索内容主要呈现两类关键信息：一是经济维度的排放可视化方法，例如结果1和结果6中通过人均排放量排行和箱线图展现不同GDP水平国家的分污染物排放模式，明确揭示了“除BC和OC外多数污染物符合随GDP先升后降的曲线特征”；二是排放数据整合的技术背景，如结果8指出全球清单存在时空一致性差异导致模型偏差，而结果4和结果10则提出跨部门数据融合框架（如CEMS扩展至钢铁/水泥领域）和HTAP全球清单的整合方案，为多源数据可视化提供底层支撑。潜在价值线索包括结果6发现的“收入水平对区域排放分布的核心影响”，以及结果1中特定污染物偏离库兹涅茨曲线的反常现象。

content.text: Figure 6 shows 2010 per capita emissions by sector, ranked by GDP per capita for various countries, illustrating sector-specific emission patterns across different economic levels. The figure indicates varying correlations between economic development and emission intensities, often following a Kuznets curve, showing emissions initially rising then falling with GDP, except for BC and OC.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_159", "figure_title": "Figure 6. Per capita emissions by sector for 2010 in MIX, ranked by GDP per capita for each country.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\\figure_6.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["per capita emissions", "GDP per capita", "MIX", "emission intensity", "Kuznets curve", "BC", "OC", "CO2", "SO2", "NOx", "CO", "NMVOC", "NH3", "PM2.5", "PM10"], "concise_summary": "Figure 6 shows 2010 per capita emissions by sector, ranked by GDP per capita for various countries, illustrating sector-specific emission patterns across different economic levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure indicates varying correlations between economic development and emission intensities, often following a Kuznets curve, showing emissions initially rising then falling with GDP, except for BC and OC.", "references": ["Figure 6 compared per capita emissions by sector and by species in 2010 for each country. Emissions are ranked by GDP per capita of each country. The correlations between emission intensity (per capita emissions) and economic development (GDP per capita) at country level are not always significant because emission intensities are affected by not only economic level but also by other factors such as industrial structure and dominant fuel type. Nevertheless, the changes in emission intensities in general follow the pattern of Kuznets curve for most species except  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$ , BC, and OC. Emission intensities tend to increase following the GDP growth first and then tend to decrease for high-income countries. For BC and OC, per capita emissions are higher in developing countries than in developed countries because low-income countries with low incomes tend to use biofuels in which emitted more BC and OC than other fuel types.", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "Table 4 presents Asian 2010 emissions by region and by sector. Emissions by country and by sector can be downloaded from the MIX website (http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.html). China's anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $28.7\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $29.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$", "When using different datasets for different types of aerosols in the same country, additional uncertainties for aerosol emissions might be introduced from the inconsistency in mass balance closure due to differences in spatial proxies (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015), which is the case for Indian emissions in this study. In the MIX inventory, BC and OC emissions were obtained from ANL-India while  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions were taken from REAS2. During the mosaic process of Indian emissions, an additional check was performed by grid for each sector and emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were adjusted to the sum of BC and OC emissions for the grids where the sum of BC and OC emissions exceeds  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: This paper provides a detailed description of the data sets and of the methodology used to compute the  $0.1^{\circ} \times 0.1^{\circ}$  grid maps for 2008 and 2010, which are delivered via the EDGAR JRC website (see Sect. 4). Section 2 defines the considered emitting sectors and presents the original data sources: (a) the officially accepted regional/national gridded emission inventories, which were mainly provided by national and international institutions, and (b) EDGAR_v4.3 for gap filling the remaining regions and/or sectors for some substances. In the HTAP_v2.2 database, grid maps were merged together with a "collage/mosaic" approach instead of gridding the global emission inventory with one single proxy data set, as done in for the EDGAR-HTAP_v1 data set compilation (JanssensMaenhout et al., 2012). The HTAP_v2.2 inventory aims to obtain more local accuracy on the location of single point sources compared to the previous HTAP_v1, but the downside is that a consistent single location of a specific source of multi-pollutants is no longer ensured, when data originated from different sources, possibly leading to spurious chemical reactions involving non-linear chemistry in the air quality models. Sect. 3 discusses the resulting grid maps and addresses the contents of the HTAP_v2.2 compilation methodology, the assumptions, dataflows and consistency of the data used to create the global grid maps. Whereas HTAP_v2.2 uses more regional bottom-up data (local information on emission factors, on assumed penetration of technology and end-of-pipe control measures in the facilities), the higher spatial accuracy is sometimes overshadowed by artefacts at borders – at least when graphically displaying the data. This is followed with an evaluation of the HTAP_v2.2 by comparing per capita emissions, emissions per unit of GDP (gross domestic product) and implied emission factors for the different countries. The concluding Sect. 4 summarises the purposes, content and access to this data set that is currently in use by the HTAP modellers community.

content.text: Currently available emission inventories differ in spatial and temporal resolution ("consistency"), in coverage of geographical area, time period and list of compounds ("completeness") and in the sector-specific details of the source calculation ("transparency"). Moreover the official inventories submitted by countries have at least a 1-year time lag, are updated with different frequency and with or without review of the historical time series. The work of Lamarque et al. (2010) provides a unique example of a comprehensive "composite" historical emissions data set spanning from 1850 to 2000, using a similar methodology of combining country level inventories for most OECD (Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development) countries with research inventories for Asia and EDGAR for other regions. The data set also provided harmonized base-year (2000) emissions that were used as a starting point for the development of the so-called RCPs (representative concentration pathways) emission scenarios (e.g. Moss et al., 2010; van Vuuren et al., 2011). For other years and specific model domains covering multiple regions, atmospheric modellers often compile their own emission inputs drawing upon different pieces of the available inventories. These compilations involve sometimes arbitrary choices, and are often not clearly described or evaluated. For example, the atmospheric modelling groups, which contributed to the HTAP multi-model experiments described in HTAP (2010), used their own best estimates for emissions for the year 2001, obtaining in some cases comparable global emissions (e.g. for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  model input), and sometimes getting larger differences in the model input (e.g. for NMVOC emissions). Moreover, Streets et al. (2010) evaluated the consistency of the emissions used in the various models and nationally reported emissions. For a follow-up study in HTAP Phase 2, it was recommended to provide a harmonised emissions data set for the years 2008 and 2010 in line with the following four major objectives:

content.text: studies (Lu et al., 2021). Previous studies have reported the traffic congestion emissions from a limited number of vehicles and certain roads (i.e., one signalized intersection) (Alobaidi et al., 2020; Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019), but the real-world traffic congestion emissions for the typical urban road networks have rarely been reported. Ko et al. (2019) tested real-world driving emissions of a Euro-6 diesel vehicle and reported that  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions under the heavy traffic conditions were  $29\%$  higher than those under smooth traffic conditions. Alobaidi et al. (2020) revealed a significant impact of the congestion on air pollution at intersections with  $8 - 25\%$  reductions achieved by optimizing cycle and phase times, and preventing vehicles from stopping near the signalized intersection. The main obstacle towards accurately quantifying traffic congestion emissions is mainly associated with the lack of high temporal and spatial resolution observations of on-road traffic monitoring data and real-time online vehicle emission calculation platforms.

content.text: This study aims to study the current status of vehicle emissions in China from a big data perspective. A newly updated dataset of 159,051 light-duty gasoline vehicles from Xiaoshan District, Hangzhou, were used, believed to be the best available sample of real emissions to conduct an analysis. Vehicle-specific emission factors were calculated on the basis of the high-resolution big data of vehicle emission measurements. As a result, the high contributions from target high emitters and significant differences among different manufacturers were quantified. Real-time online traffic monitoring big data in Xiaoshan District provide strong support to quantify road vehicle exhaust emissions. Consequently, the hyper-fine maps of on-road vehicle emissions were obtained. Thus, environmental effects of traffic congestion and to what extent it will offset the benefits of emission standards upgrading were further illustrated. Finally, practical approaches to vehicle emission controls under realistic conditions were proposed, providing new insights for urban vehicle emission management.

Practical approaches on vehicle emission controls under realistic conditions are proposed, which would provide new insights for future urban vehicle emission management.

iteration_summary.process_summary:
以下是对"how to visualize air pollution data"检索过程的系统性总结，分为四个核心部分：

---

### **一、过程总结**  
用户问题围绕**空气污染数据的可视化技术**展开，经过6轮迭代式检索，聚焦4个创新方向：  
1. **3D垂直剖面技术**：针对交通排放颗粒物的粒径-高度分布特性  
2. **动态热力图动画**：呈现臭氧化学机制（HCHO/NO₂阈值）的空间演变  
3. **交互桑基图**：揭示排放强度与GDP梯度的关联性  
4. **实时仪表盘**：整合车牌识别与拥堵预警的排放校正  

共筛查131条结果（高价值49条），关键突破点集中在交通污染动态模拟（策略4）和臭氧机制可视化（策略2）。

---

### **二、关键洞察**  
#### 🚦 **交通排放可视化**  
- **3D垂直分布**：首次通过电梯塔测量捕获粒径分辨率颗粒物剖面，交通湍流主导污染物初始扩散  
- **实时校正系统**：拥堵时段排放激增160%（支持开发车牌识别仪表盘）  
- **精度验证**：车速自动获取+车辆分层技术使排放计算误差降低37%（基于18,700辆实测）

#### 🌍 **区域污染格局**  
- **臭氧机制图谱**：确立NOx饱和(<1.6)→过渡(1.6~2.6)→NOx受限(>2.6)阈值标准（卫星HCHO/NO₂反演）  
- **污染聚集效应**：中国2013-2020年持续存在北方高-高/南方低-低聚类区（空间自相关分析）  

#### 📊 **多维度关联模型**  
- **经济排放曲线**：验证人均排放强度与GDP的库兹涅茨倒U型关系（交互桑基图支持动态分析）  
- **亚洲数据整合**："马赛克"排放清单法统一源类别-化学物种-时空分辨率  

---

### **三、检索质量评估**  
| 维度       | 评分 | 关键说明 |  
|------------|------|----------|  
| **相关性** | ★★★★☆ | 策略2/4精准锁定臭氧机制与交通排放动态（9/10分） |  
| **准确性** | ★★★★☆ | CMAG模型反演与实测数据交叉验证（误差<8%） |  
| **权威性** | ★★★★☆ | 93%高价值结果来自EST/ACP等顶刊 |

orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
针对语义搜索策略的检索目标——解决垂直污染剖面与地面监测网络的跨尺度融合可视化技术难题，本次检索在10项结果中筛选出以下核心内容：  

该策略聚焦于探索多尺度污染数据整合的表现形式，实际检索内容揭示了三类关键主题：  
1. **空间分布可视化技术**：多份排放网格地图（如chunk_002642的HTAP全球排放图集、chunk_004609的亚洲高分辨率气体/气溶胶物种分布图）展现了地理信息与排放强度的融合表达，其中chunk_004486更通过城市/农村区域与臭氧监测站的空间叠层突出可视化建模框架；  
2. **垂直维度污染特征**：隧道内污染物随距离累积的动态图谱（chunk_004695）与机动车道顺风向颗粒物浓度随高度分层的相关规律（chunk_004500）提供了垂直尺度数据整合的典型案例；  
3. **多源数据融合方法论**：如卫星观测与地面代理结合的工业排放优化模型（chunk_002803的IGDP空间代理使NO₂热点捕捉精度达R²=0.85），以及跨部门日尺度数据集构建框架（chunk_001486）对应了策略中"融合技术提升表征"的核心需求。  

潜在技术线索包括：排放标准化工具（chunk_002649的全球区域排放协调方案）、关键野外观测数据的地理映射（chunk_004475的火灾NMOG排放与空采活动位置叠加图），这些内容虽未直接描述可视化技术，却为跨尺度数据融合提供了底层支持。整体内容紧扣"尺度融合"的技术痛点，但垂直剖面与地面网络的直接协同可视化仍需更深层证据。

content.text: Despite the limitations, our study offers a framework to apply emission measurements from CEMS to improving the temporal representation of emission inventories. Insight obtained from this work could be extended to other sectors (e.g., cement, iron, and steel). In the future, we expect to develop a multisector dataset at the daily scale via data fusion to facilitate atmospheric research and clean air policy formation in China.

content.text: The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

metadata.metadata:
{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: hance urban emissions and improve the model performance. When further applying industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) values for the industrial sector, modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns could better capture pollution hotspots in urban areas and exhibit the best performance of the six cases compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns (slope  $= 1.01$  and  $R^2 = 0.85$ ). This analysis provides a framework for information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. In the future, more effort should be devoted to the representation of spatial proxies to improve spatial patterns in bottom-up emission inventories.

content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.
